description: Start with the bat on your shoulder and focus on a rhythmic three-step load process. On "one," shift your weight slightly back; on "two," lift your lead leg into a stride; and on "three," plant and swing smoothly. This drill is designed to help hitters find a repeatable tempo and avoid a static or rushed approach at the plate.

Set up a ball on the tee and use a rhythmic "rock back, rock forward, and swing" motion to create momentum. The goal is to synchronize the hand load with the stride to ensure the body is moving in unison before making contact. It emphasizes fluid movement over a rigid, mechanical swing.

Place four targets or cones in the corners of the bunting zone and attempt to deaden the ball into each specific area. Focus on "catching" the ball with the barrel and using your legs to adjust to different heights rather than just your arms. This improves bat control and the ability to execute situational bunts under pressure.

A coach tosses seven balls in rapid succession to different areas of the strike zone, requiring the hitter to reset quickly between swings. This drill builds conditioning, improves hand-eye coordination, and forces the hitter to maintain short, compact paths to the ball as fatigue sets in.

Position the tee at three different angles relative to the hitter's stance to practice directional hitting. Swing at balls set up for pull-side (90), up the middle (even), and opposite field (45) to learn how the contact point changes for each. This teaches the hitter how to let the ball travel or meet it out front based on location.

The coach sits at a 45-degree angle to the hitter and flips balls toward the plate, forcing the hitter to stay "inside" the ball to drive it back toward the coach. This prevents "pulling off" the ball and encourages a path that stays through the hitting zone for as long as possible.

drillName: Angled Force Plate Tee - Back Foot
description: Place a force plate or an angled block under the back foot to emphasize pushing off the inside of the foot rather than collapsing the ankle. This creates a stronger foundation and helps the hitter transfer energy from the ground up through the core. It is excellent for hitters who struggle with "spinning out" on their back side.

Place a small plastic cone just behind the hitter's back foot to serve as a physical boundary. If the hitter's heel or weight collapses backward during the swing, they will tip the cone, providing immediate feedback on balance. This encourages a vertical axis and proper hip rotation without losing ground.

Focus on the "hinge" of the back hip by pausing at the peak of the load before swinging at the tee. The hitter should feel tension in the glute and hip, ensuring they are "loaded" and ready to explode forward. This prevents a premature leak of energy and builds a more powerful lower half.

Set the tee up with a net placed just behind the hitter’s back shoulder, forcing a tight, "hand-led" turn. If the hitter "casts" their hands or has a long "loop" in their swing, the bat will strike the net behind them. This drill is essential for developing a short, compact swing path to the ball.

The tosser stands behind the hitter and flips the ball into the hitting zone from behind the shoulder. The hitter must track the ball as it enters their peripheral vision and react quickly, which improves "late" tracking and forces a direct path to the ball.

Perform tee work while standing on a 2x4 wooden beam or a specialized balance rail to challenge stability. If the hitter reaches too far or falls off balance during the follow-through, it indicates a flaw in their center of gravity. This builds a rock-solid foundation and core control.

The coach tosses the ball specifically to the bottom of the strike zone, requiring the hitter to stay in their legs and maintain a proper spine angle. This prevents "reaching" with the hands and teaches the hitter how to elevate low pitches by getting the barrel underneath the ball correctly.

The coach holds two balls and yells "Ball" or "Hit" just before the toss; if "Hit" is called, the hitter swings, but if "Ball" is called, the hitter must take the pitch. This sharpens cognitive processing and discipline, ensuring the hitter is tracking the ball all the way into the zone.

Hold the bat horizontally against the small of the back, hooked into the elbows, and practice dry rotations toward a tee. This drill isolates the lower body and core, showing the hitter how the hips should lead the swing without relying on the arms to create movement.

Combine the 1-2-3 rhythm drill with a toss coming from behind the hitter's back hip. The hitter counts "one, two" as they load, and the coach tosses on "three," forcing the hitter to react to a ball they didn't see until the last moment. This builds elite reaction time and hand speed.

Place the tee at the height of the hitter's navel to practice hitting the "belt-high" strike. Hitters often struggle with this height by either "chopping" down or "looping" up; this drill reinforces a flat, powerful plane through the middle of the zone.

Choke up on the bat and use only the lead hand to swing at the ball on the tee. This strengthens the "pulling" motion of the swing and ensures the lead arm doesn't "wing out" or become disconnected from the body. It is vital for maintaining a direct path to the ball.

The coach mixes in slower, higher-arc tosses with standard front flips without warning. The hitter must recognize the speed difference and "wait" with their lower half, avoiding the common mistake of lunging forward. This builds "stay back" discipline and adjustability.

Set up two tees at different heights and depths, and have a partner call out which one to hit just as the hitter starts their stride. This forces the hitter to adjust their mechanics mid-motion, simulating the timing change required to hit an off-speed pitch.

Use balls with different colored dots or seams and have the hitter call out the color before making contact. This forces the eyes to focus intensely on the ball rather than just "zoning out," which significantly improves tracking and contact quality.

Stand on a slight downward slope (or use a decline board) to practice staying back against gravity. This prevents the hitter from "rushing" down the mound and teaches them how to keep their weight centered over their back leg even when the body wants to drift forward.

Practice "taking" pitches while in a full hitting stance, focusing on the ability to stay balanced and "track" the ball into the catcher's mitt without flinching. This builds "strike zone awareness" and helps hitters realize they don't have to swing at everything they see.

The coach tosses two balls at once, one high and one low, and calls out "High" or "Low" after the balls are in the air. The hitter must quickly identify the correct target and execute a swing, which develops rapid decision-making and hand-eye coordination.

Set two tees up in a line, one in front of the other. The hitter must swing and hit the front ball without knocking over the back tee, or vice-versa. This reinforces a specific swing plane, teaching the hitter how to "level out" the barrel at the right moment.

Place two tees at different locations (inner and outer). As the hitter begins their load, the coach shouts "Inside" or "Outside," and the hitter must adjust their hands and path to hit the corresponding ball. This simulates the split-second adjustments needed in a real at-bat.

Place the tee significantly further out in front of the plate than usual to practice "extension." The hitter focuses on hitting through the ball and reaching a full "V" shape with their arms after contact, ensuring they aren't "cutting off" their swing too early.

The coach throws firmer, flatter tosses from a shorter distance to simulate high-velocity pitching. This reduces the hitter's reaction time and forces them to eliminate any "extra" movements in their load, promoting a "short to the ball, long through the ball" philosophy.

drillName: Flaw- Casting the Hands Outside of the Ball
description: To fix this, place a tee or a soft barrier on the outside of the plate and practice hitting balls on the inside corner. The hitter must keep their hands "tucked" and lead with the knob to avoid hitting the barrier, effectively curing the "long" swing path.

Place a tee at the top of the strike zone and focus on hitting "line drives" rather than ground balls. The hitter must feel the barrel stay "on plane" with the ball rather than attacking it at a steep downward angle, which often results in weak contact.

Place a tall tee or a PVC pipe behind the hitter’s back side to keep the spine vertical. The hitter practices rotating "around" their axis rather than "dipping" their back shoulder, which helps maintain a level swing and prevents popping up.

drillName: Flaw- Contact Point too Far Out Front
description: Move the tee back toward the hitter's back foot and practice driving the ball to the opposite field. This teaches the hitter to "let the ball travel" and realize they can still generate power even when the ball is deep in the zone.

Place a "high tee" in the zone and require the hitter to hit line drives. If the hands drop, the hitter will swing "under" the ball or hit the tee itself. This encourages a more direct, downward-to-level hand path from the starting position.

Use a mirror or video analysis to check that the feet are shoulder-width apart, knees are athletic, and hands are in a "loaded" position. Repeatedly stepping into the box and "checking" these markers builds the muscle memory needed for a consistent starting point.

Use a resistance band anchored behind the hitter and held in the lead hand. As the hitter strides forward, they must feel the band "stretch" as the hands stay back. This creates the "rubber band effect" necessary for elite bat speed and power.

Perform the "Wall Drill" where the hitter stands with their back a bat-length away from a wall. During the swing, if the barrel "loops" or "drags," it will strike the wall. This forces a "knob-first" entry into the hitting zone.

Practice hitting off a tee while keeping the front heel off the ground until the last possible second. This forces the weight to stay on the "inside" of the back leg, preventing the common "lunge" where the head moves too far forward before the swing.

Practice "Happy Gilmore" style swings where the hitter steps behind their lead foot and then forward into the swing. This exaggerated movement forces the hips to engage and creates a feeling of "driving" through the ball using the large muscles of the legs.

Place a firm object or a "slant board" under the front foot to encourage a "firm front side" upon impact. A "soft" or bent front knee leaks power; this drill helps the hitter "post up" and transfer all their energy into the barrel.

Place a bat or a 2x4 on the ground behind the hitter's lead foot. If the hitter "steps in the bucket" (steps toward third base for a righty), they will trip or step on the object. This reinforces a straight, "toward the pitcher" stride path.

Focus entirely on the finish of the swing, ensuring the bat finishes high and the chest is facing the pitcher. The hitter should be able to "hold" their finish for three seconds without wobbling, indicating perfect balance throughout the entire motion.

The tosser stands in front and slightly to the side, flipping balls toward the hitter's front hip. This requires the hitter to "clear" their hips quickly and stay "inside" a ball that is "crowding" them, which is essential for hitting inside fastballs.

Have the hitter throw the ball up to themselves and hit it into the field, just like a coach. This build "hand-eye-barrel" coordination and teaches the hitter how to "time up" their own body movements to make solid contact.

The hitter takes a swing but stops the barrel exactly at the "point of contact" and holds it. This allows the coach and player to check the "checkpoints": is the back knee bent? Are the eyes on the ball? Is the "V" in the hands correct? It’s a great diagnostic tool.

Set the tee at the very top of the strike zone (chest height). The hitter must stay "on top" of the ball and drive line drives. This is the ultimate "anti-loop" drill and forces the hitter to use their hands effectively to get the barrel to a high pitch.

The hitter stands with their back foot on an inflatable balance disc while hitting off a tee. The instability forces the hitter to engage their core and glutes to stay balanced, reinforcing a powerful and stable "backside" load.

The coach tosses a ball and the hitter must put it on the ground, preferably toward the right side of the field. This simulates the "hit and run" play where contact and direction are more important than power, teaching bat control and situational awareness.

Set up two tees—one on the inner half and one on the outer half. The hitter alternates swings, focusing on "pulling" the inside ball and "driving" the outside ball to the opposite gap. This teaches the different "contact points" required for different pitch locations.

During the load, the hitter brings their lead knee back until it almost touches their back knee before striding forward. This "exaggerated load" ensures the weight is fully gathered on the back side and helps hitters who have a "short" or "non-existent" stride.

The hitter focuses on "pointing the knob" of the bat at the ball as it is tossed. This prevents the "casting" motion and ensures the hands stay "inside" the ball's path, leading to better "barrel-to-ball" accuracy and more "pop."

Move the tee to various spots in and around the strike zone (low and away, high and tight, etc.) without the hitter looking. The hitter must then step in, identify the location, and execute the correct swing for that specific spot.

Place the tee on the home plate and aim for a target deep in the outfield (like the centerfield fence). This encourages the hitter to "stay through" the ball and use their full "extension" to maximize distance, rather than just "slapping" at the ball.

Set the tee as low as it can go, near the knees. The hitter must "sink" into their legs and maintain their "spine angle" to reach the ball without "bending at the waist." This builds leg strength and the ability to drive low pitches.

Use a shortened "one-hand" bat to hit balls off a low tee. The smaller bat requires much more precision and forces the hitter to use their "small muscles" in the wrists and hands to control the barrel, while the low height keeps the legs engaged.

The hitter starts with their feet already in the "wide" stride position and hits off the tee using only the rotation of their hips and hands. This isolates the "turn" and is excellent for hitters who have "too much head movement" during their stride.

The coach uses a "long" arm circle and a slow release to simulate a curveball or change-up. The hitter must recognize the "slower" motion and hold their "hinge" longer, teaching them how to not "over-commit" to the fastball.

Alternate between "Top Hand Only" and "Bottom Hand Only" swings using a short bat. This isolates the specific roles of each hand (bottom hand pulls, top hand directs/powers) and identifies which hand might be "weaker" or "dominant" in the swing.

The coach tosses the ball from a side angle so it "fades" away from the hitter. This forces the hitter to "reach" with their hands and "stay on" the ball, teaching them how to drive the "away" pitch into the opposite-field gap.

Set the tee on the "outer black" of the plate and slightly "deeper" in the zone. The hitter focuses on hitting the "inside half" of the ball and driving it to the opposite field, reinforcing the "let it travel" mindset.

Use a "heavy bat" or a weighted "donut" during soft toss to build "functional strength" in the forearms and core. The extra weight forces the hitter to use "perfect mechanics" to move the bat, as "muscling it" with just the arms becomes too difficult.

The coach varies the speed, location, and "timing" of the tosses with no predictable pattern. This is the most "game-like" form of front toss and forces the hitter to "react" rather than "anticipate," which is the core of successful hitting.

The coach flips balls one after another as fast as the hitter can reset. This "burnout" drill builds "fast-twitch" muscle fibers and forces the hitter to find the most "efficient" path to the ball to keep up with the pace.

The hitter starts with their "front foot" back and "steps forward" into the box as the ball is tossed. This creates "forward momentum" and helps hitters who are "stagnant" or "too quiet" in their load to feel what "athletic movement" feels like.

Combine a "rhythmic sway" or "bat waggle" with the tee swing to ensure the hitter isn't "tight." A "relaxed" hitter is a "fast" hitter; this drill emphasizes "loose muscles" and "fluid motion" before the explosive "turn."

As the hitter swings, they "cross" their back leg behind their front leg (the scissor move). This move, often seen in elite hitters on "low and away" pitches, ensures the "hips clear" and the "weight stays back," preventing a "lunge."

The hitter pauses at three points: "Stance," "Load/Stride," and "Swing." During the "Load" pause, the coach checks for "separation" (hands back, foot forward). This "static-to-dynamic" drill ensures the "stretch" is present before the launch.

drillName: Short Bat Bottom Hand Front Toss
description: Using a "one-hand" mini bat and only the "bottom hand," the hitter reacts to front tosses. This is "advanced" hand-eye coordination work and builds an incredibly "strong" lead arm that won't "collapse" on high-velocity pitches.

Similar to the toss drill, but the "static" ball on the tee allows the hitter to focus purely on the "path" of the lead arm. The goal is to "sweep" the barrel through the zone while keeping the "elbow tucked" to the ribcage.

drillName: Short Bat Top Hand Front Toss
description: Using the "top hand" only on a mini bat during front toss. The top hand is responsible for the "palm up" position at contact and "driving" through the ball; this drill builds the "punching" power needed for home run strength.

Focus on the "top hand" path off the tee. The hitter should feel like they are "throwing the barrel" at the ball. It is an excellent drill for hitters who "drag" their hands or have a "lazy" top hand that doesn't "fire."

The classic "soft toss" from the side. The key is to toss the ball "into the hitter's contact point" rather than just "at them." This allows for high-volume repetition of "barrel accuracy" and "rhythm."

A general term for any toss coming from the side or a 45-degree angle. It is best used for "warm-ups" or to "work on a specific feel" (like "staying inside" or "finishing high") before moving to faster, "front-on" pitching.

The hitter uses their bat to measure the distance from the "plate" to their "feet" and from "heel to heel." Establishing these "personal metrics" ensures that the hitter "sets up" in the exact same spot every single time they enter the box.

Place a specific "target" (like a net pocket or a cone) in the field and try to hit the ball off the tee into that target. This "external focus" often helps hitters "stop overthinking" their mechanics and just "let their athleticism" take over.

The coach stands on the "mound" and goes through a full "windup" while the hitter waits at the tee. The hitter must "time" their "load and stride" to the coach's "release," then swing at the ball on the tee. This builds "pitcher-hitter" synchronization.

Set up three "home plates" at different distances from the coach. The hitter moves from "plate to plate" between tosses, which changes the "perceived velocity" and "angle" of the toss, forcing constant "re-calibration" of the eyes.

Using an old bat, the hitter goes through a full swing but literally "releases" the bat toward a "target" in the field (like a net). This promotes "full extension" and "release of tension," as you can't "throw" a bat effectively if you are "aiming" it.

Place the tee as close to the hitter's "belt" as possible. The hitter must "clear" their hands and "turn" their hips extremely fast to avoid "getting jammed." This is the best drill for "learning to hit the inside fastball."

The coach stands on a chair or ladder and "drops" a ball into the hitting zone. The hitter must "react" and "swing" before the ball hits the ground. This develops "elite" hand speed and a "short, direct" path to the ball.

Using a "full-sized" bat but only the "top hand" to hit off the tee. This is a "strength" and "control" drill; the hitter must maintain the "palm up" position and "drive" the ball without the "help" of the lead arm's "pulling" force.

The coach tosses two balls "stacked" on top of each other. The hitter tries to hit "both" balls or is told "Top" or "Bottom" mid-toss. This requires "extreme" barrel precision and "focus" on a very small "target area."

Use a "lightweight" bat (like a fungo or a "skinny bat") during soft toss to increase "hand speed." The goal is to "swing as fast as possible" while maintaining "form," which "over-trains" the central nervous system for "explosiveness."

Place a volleyball or a "foam block" between the hitter's knees during tee work. The hitter must "squeeze" the ball and keep it in place during the "turn," which prevents the "knees from bowing out" and ensures a "tight, powerful" rotation.

Toss "heavy" sand-filled balls (like Plyo balls) to the hitter. Because the balls are "heavy," the hitter must "hit through" the ball with their "entire body"; weak "arm swings" will result in the ball simply "dropping" to the ground.

The hitter takes "short, choppy" swings at the tee using "only their wrists." This "isolates" the "forearm and wrist" muscles, which are the "last link" in the "power chain" and are responsible for that "final snap" of the barrel.
